steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what "specific" most nearly means as used in the passage. Moss has never tired of this view — the word singles out one particular view (from the elevated track above West Oakland) as distinct from any other view he might have.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — It suggests that a commonly held belief regarding an organism is mistaken.: The view is real and physically described; nothing is fictional or invented. ✗
  • Choice B — It summarizes the history of research into a biological phenomenon.: "Correct" implies truth or accuracy, not the sense of picking out one distinct thing from others. ✗
  • Choice C — It illustrates why one organism may pose more of a threat than another.: Describes liveliness or action; the word modifies a view, not a person or movement. ✗
  • Choice D — It explains a practical benefit of researching a particular biological process.: Captures exactly the sense of this one, unique, identifiable view — as opposed to views in general. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
